title: [Tool] Structural duplicates in React codebase (34.1% of .ts/.tsx files affected)
author: DecSec2030
body:
## Structural duplicates in React codebase (34.1% of .ts/.tsx files affected)

I ran [find-duplicates](https://github.com/DecSec2030/find-duplicates) — a tool that detects structural code duplicates (normalizing identifiers, strings, and whitespace before comparing) — on the main branch at `c0c39a6`.

### Summary

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| .ts/.tsx files scanned | 521 |
| Files with duplicates | 178 (34.1%) |
| Duplicate groups | 342 |
| Duplicated blocks | 973 |

### By directory

| Directory | Duplicates | Files |
|-----------|-----------|-------|
| `compiler/packages/` | 816 | 165 |
| `packages/eslint-plugin-react-hooks/` | 85 | 6 |
| `compiler/scripts/` | 64 | 5 |
| `packages/react/` | 6 | 1 |
| `fixtures/` | 2 | 1 |

### Notable findings

**1. `compiler/packages/snap/src/minimize.ts` — 30 near-identical generator methods (142 duplicates)**

Functions like `removeStatements`, `removeCallArguments`, `removeFunctionParameters`, `simplifyCallExpressions`, etc. all follow the exact same pattern: count AST nodes of a type → loop with clone → traverse → replace. This is the single largest source of duplication.

**2. `compiler/scripts/ts-compile-fixture.ts` — 14 identical pipeline passes (64 duplicates)**

Each pipeline pass checks `if (env.hasErrors()) { return formatEnvError(...); }` with near-identical structure across all passes.

**3. `compiler/packages/babel-plugin-react-compiler/` — scattered scope/binding checks (15 files)**

A pattern of checking bindings and scope ranges (`if (binding) { ... }`) is repeated across 15 different files in the compiler, suggesting a candidate for a shared utility.

**4. Core `packages/react/` is clean — only 6 duplicate blocks in 1 file.**

The mature React core has very few structural duplicates, which is expected for a well-refactored codebase.

### Context

The vast majority of duplicates are in `compiler/` (React Forget), a newer, actively-developed package. The core React packages are essentially clean.

### Options

- `compiler/packages/snap/src/minimize.ts` — could extract a `createGenerator(pattern)` helper to eliminate the 30 repeated method bodies
- Pipeline passes — could use a higher-order function for the `hasErrors()` check
- Shared binding helpers — consolidate the scattered pattern into `Utils/binding.ts`

---

*Generated with [find-duplicates](https://github.com/DecSec2030/find-duplicates) v1.1.0 (Go binary, 3.4 MB)*

title: [DevTools Bug]: confused on prerender pages and resulting in high-cpu usage loop
author: phistuck
body:
### Website or app

example; https://yuri.twintail.org/chrome/prerendering/specrules.html

### Repro steps

Just like the original issue (https://github.com/react/react/issues/35954), although I am not Takashi/from the team -
> Hello, I'm Takashi from Google Chrome team.
> 
> I've investigated high-cpu usage issue on prerendering, and noticed that the React Developer Tools seems to be a root cause.
> The original report for the chromium project is here; https://crbug.com/478909972
> 
> Steps:
> 1. Install React Developer Tools Extension (from the Chrome Web Store)
> 2. Visit a page that uses specupationrules
> 3. Open Chrome's Task manager from ... > More tools >, and see CPU usages
> 4. Usage for the prerender pages shows higher CPU usages
> 5. Open DevTools
> 6. Prerender pages' CPU usages get much higher, and frame of the React Developer Tools spends almost 100%
> 
> This needs that the current page is accessible from the Extensions. e.g., they would not have a permission to touch the New Tab Page, and this doesn't happen there.
> 
> Also, this happens with Chrome UI triggered pretenders too. So, even if the page doesn't use the speculationrules, the problem could happen when users type something in the Omnibox, and Chrome has confidence on the next navigating page.
> 
> I suspect this is because the extension doesn't expect the Chrome's recent MPArch: Multi-Page Architecture, and is confused on multiple page navigations.
> Here is a resource for the prerender and link for its impact on the Extension system.
> [Prerender pages in Chrome for instant page navigations > Impact on extensions
> ](https://developer.chrome.com/docs/web-platform/prerender-pages#impact_on_extensions)



### How often does this bug happen?

Every time

title: [Compiler Bug]: React Compiler keeps "new Date().getTime()" constant across component re-renders
author: aneesijaz

title: [Compiler Bug]: MobX observer component is compiled without an incompatible-library diagnostic and loses reactivity
author: BranZhang
body:
### What kind of issue is this?

- [x] React Compiler core (the JS output is incorrect, or your app works incorrectly after optimization)
- [ ] babel-plugin-react-compiler (build issue installing or using the Babel plugin)
- [ ] eslint-plugin-react-hooks (build issue installing or using the eslint plugin)
- [ ] react-compiler-healthcheck (build issue installing or using the healthcheck script)

### Link to repro

https://github.com/BranZhang/react-compiler-mobx-repro

### Repro steps

## Summary

When `babel-plugin-react-compiler` is enabled with `compilationMode: 'infer'`, a MobX `observer` component declared as a top-level function and then wrapped with `observer(Component)` can keep returning stale JSX after observable changes.

The failure is silent: no runtime error or compiler warning is shown. The UI keeps rendering the initial value after the MobX store changes.

## Reproduction

Repository: https://github.com/BranZhang/react-compiler-mobx-repro

```bash
npm install
npm run dev
```

Open `http://localhost:5199`, then click `store.increment()` three times.

## Actual Behavior

The rows using a top-level function declaration wrapped by `observer(...)` keep displaying `0`:

- `plain observer: 0`
- `observer + console.log: 0`

The MobX store value does change, and other subscribers can observe the updated value.

## Expected Behavior

The observed component should continue to update when `COUNTER_STORE.count` changes, or React Compiler should skip this pattern / warn that it is unsafe.

## Control Cases

The same reproduction includes several control cases:

- `observer + window write` updates to `3`, because the render-time global assignment makes React Compiler bail out.
- `observer + 'use no memo'` updates to `3`, because the component is skipped by React Compiler.
- `reaction + useState` updates to `3`, because it uses an explicit MobX subscription instead of render-time `observer` tracking.
- Commenting out `babel-plugin-react-compiler` in `vite.config.ts` makes all rows update correctly.
- Changing `const Plain = observer(PlainComponent)` to an inline function expression passed to `observer(...)` also makes the row update correctly.

## Suspected Mechanism

`observer` relies on executing the component function during render inside a MobX tracking context. Observable reads that actually happen during render become the component subscription.

The compiled output shows that `COUNTER_STORE.count` is still read on every render, so MobX continues tracking the observable and notifying the component. However, React Compiler caches the JSX created during the initial render without treating the observable value as a dependency. After a store update:

1. MobX triggers a re-render.
2. The component reads the latest `COUNTER_STORE.count`, so the MobX subscription remains active.
3. The compiled memo cache returns the JSX created during the initial render.
4. The component therefore keeps displaying the initial value even though it continues to re-render with updated observable values.

## Versions

- react: 19.2.0
- react-dom: 19.2.0
- mobx: 6.13.3
- mobx-react: 9.2.2
- mobx-react-lite: 4.1.1
- babel-plugin-react-compiler: 1.0.0
- vite: 5.4

### How often does this bug happen?

Every time

### What version of React are you using?

19.2.0

### What version of React Compiler are you using?

babel-plugin-react-compiler@1.0.0

title: Page hangs forever at 100% CPU inside the Fizz batched-reveal runtime (`$RV`) — `insertBefore` loop never terminates (React 19.2, reproduces in both Chrome and Safari)
author: inxx
body:
On a production e-commerce page rendered with streaming SSR (Next.js App Router + PPR, ~15 streamed Suspense boundaries), the browser main thread intermittently locks up **forever** during page load: 100% CPU, memory growing to multiple GBs, page stays blank/white. The renderer never returns to the event loop (Chrome DevTools cannot even attach/evaluate). We captured the exact location twice, in two different engines — see **The current behavior** below.

React version: 19.2.4 (vendored by Next.js 16.2.6, App Router, PPR enabled, `cacheComponents`). Also reproduced with the runtime shipped in Next 16.2.10 (byte-identical `$RV`).

## Steps To Reproduce

1. Serve a streaming-SSR page: Next.js App Router with PPR enabled, ~15 streamed Suspense boundaries, large SSR DOM (~1,700 nodes), and third-party scripts with active MutationObservers on the page.
2. Load the page under production-like server latency and reload repeatedly during an affected period — within ~10 reloads the main thread locks at 100% CPU, memory climbs to multiple GBs, and the page stays blank/white.

The hang is timing-dependent (segment arrival order/batching): repro rate on the same build swings between 0% and ~90% across hours, apparently tracking server response latency.

Link to code example:

No minimal repro yet — the hang depends on segment arrival timing/batching, so it does not isolate into a static sandbox. Happy to share the production URL **privately**, run instrumented builds/patches, and capture more traces (we can reproduce within ~10 reloads during affected periods). Guidance on what to instrument in `$RV`/`$RC` to pin down the exact DOM state would be appreciated.

## The current behavior

The main thread never returns to the event loop. We captured the exact location twice, in two different engines:

- **Chrome 149**: a CDP `Debugger.pause` interrupt landed inside the inline `$RV` function of the streamed document (single call frame, anonymous inline script).
- **Safari (macOS 15.7, WebKit)**: `sample` of the WebContent process shows **3664/3664** main-thread samples pinned in one stack:

```
__CFRunLoopDoTimers → WebCore::ThreadTimers → RemoteLayerTreeDrawingArea::updateRendering
→ WebCore::Page::updateRendering
→ WebCore::ScriptedAnimationController::serviceRequestAnimationFrameCallbacks
→ WebCore::JSRequestAnimationFrameCallback::invoke
→ JSC::Interpreter::executeBoundCall          ← the rAF callback is a bound function = $RV.bind(null, $RB)
→ (JIT frames)
→ WebCore::jsNodePrototypeFunction_insertBefore   (3644 / 3664 samples)
→ WebCore::ContainerNode::insertBefore            (2043 / 3664 samples)
```

The bound-function rAF callback matches how `$RC` schedules the batched reveal (`requestAnimationFrame($RV.bind(null,$RB))`), and the hot frame matches `$RV`'s content-move loop:

```js
for (; e.firstChild; ) f.insertBefore(e.firstChild, c);
```

Reproducing in **both Blink and WebKit** rules out a browser engine bug — the loop is a pure DOM-semantics issue in the inline runtime.

<details>
<summary>The runtime captured from our page</summary>

```js
$RV=function(a){$RT=performance.now();for(var b=0;b<a.length;b+=2){var c=a[b],e=a[b+1];
null!==e.parentNode&&e.parentNode.removeChild(e);var f=c.parentNode;if(f){var g=c.previousSibling,h=0;
do{if(c&&8===c.nodeType){var d=c.data;if("/$"===d||"/&"===d)if(0===h)break;else h--;
else"$"!==d&&"$?"!==d&&"$~"!==d&&"$!"!==d&&"&"!==d||h++}d=c.nextSibling;f.removeChild(c);c=d}while(c);
for(;e.firstChild;)f.insertBefore(e.firstChild,c);g.data="$";
g._reactRetry&&requestAnimationFrame(g._reactRetry)}}a.length=0};
$RC=function(a,b){if(b=document.getElementById(b))(a=document.getElementById(a))?
(a.previousSibling.data="$~",$RB.push(a,b),2===$RB.length&&("number"!==typeof $RT?
requestAnimationFrame($RV.bind(null,$RB)):(a=performance.now(),
setTimeout($RV.bind(null,$RB),2300>a&&2E3<a?2300-a:$RT+300-a)))):b.parentNode.removeChild(b)};
```
</details>

### Suspected mechanism (hypothesis)

`for(;e.firstChild;)f.insertBefore(e.firstChild,c)` terminates only if moving `e.firstChild` before `c` actually removes it from `e`. If the reference node `c` (the position where the marker walk stopped) ends up **inside `e`** — or `f === e` — the moved node remains a child of `e` and the child list cycles forever (e.g. `[A,B,c]` → move A before c → `[B,A,c]` → move B → `[A,B,c]` → …). That is an infinite loop with no allocation failure, and every `insertBefore` also queues MutationObserver records for any observers on the page (analytics/chat widgets), which explains the unbounded memory growth we see.

We statically verified the **fully-loaded** document: all 15 `$RC("B:x","S:x")` pairs have well-formed markers (close comment is a sibling of the placeholder, segment div does not contain them). So the pathological state appears to arise **dynamically** — depending on which boundaries get batched into a single `$RV` flush and how earlier reveals mutated the DOM. This matches the intermittency we observe.

## The expected behavior

The batched reveal (`$RV`) completes and returns to the event loop. If a marker walk is corrupted or raced — the reference node ends up inside the fragment being drained, or `f === e` — the runtime should degrade to a broken/failed Suspense boundary rather than hanging the entire page. A defensive fix suggestion: bound the move loop, or verify `!e.contains(c)` before the move, so a corrupted/raced marker degrades to a broken boundary instead of hanging the whole page.

## Environment

- react / react-dom: 19.2.4 (vendored by Next.js 16.2.6, App Router, PPR enabled, `cacheComponents`)
- Also reproduced with the runtime shipped in Next 16.2.10 (byte-identical `$RV`)
- Chrome 149.0.7827.201 (macOS), Safari 18.x / macOS 15.7 — both reproduce
